bugs.description: PAIR 1 of 2 (cross-hunk). The per-request `is_active` guard was removed from `get_current_user`. On its own this looks like harmless de-duplication, the same check still exists at login, so a reviewer reading only this hunk may rationalize it as redundant. But its twin removal in login.py (BUG_002) takes out the OTHER copy, so together there is no `is_active` enforcement anywhere: a deactivated/suspended user can still log in AND keep using existing tokens. The vulnerability only appears if you connect both hunks; each in isolation reads as a reasonable cleanup.

injection.replace_with:
user = session.get(User, token_data.sub)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="User not found")
    return user

bugs.description: PAIR 1 of 2 (cross-hunk). The `is_active` check was removed from `login_access_token`, so a deactivated user can obtain a fresh access token. Alone this looks defensible, the dependency layer (`get_current_user`) also checks `is_active`, so a reviewer reading only this hunk may treat it as redundant. But BUG_001 removes that other copy, so the two removals together eliminate every `is_active` gate: account deactivation is fully bypassed. Detecting it requires reasoning across both hunks, not just this one.

injection.replace_with:
if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Incorrect email or password")

bugs.description: PAIR 2 of 2 (cross-hunk). The `unique=True` constraint was dropped from the `email` column (kept `index=True`). On its own this looks like a minor schema tweak, and a reviewer may assume the application still guards uniqueness, the create-user route does a pre-check. But BUG_004 removes that route-level pre-check, so with both gone nothing prevents two accounts sharing one email: duplicate registrations, ambiguous login/lookup by email, and account-takeover risk via the password-reset-by-email flow. The defect is the interaction of the two hunks; either alone is plausibly fine.

PAIR 2 of 2 (cross-hunk). The duplicate-email pre-check (`get_user_by_email` -> raise 400 if found) was removed from `create_user`. Alone this looks acceptable, the database `email` column is unique, so a duplicate would be rejected at the DB layer anyway. But BUG_003 removes that `unique=True` constraint, so with both gone duplicate-email accounts can be created. Neither hunk alone is clearly wrong; the vulnerability is that BOTH the application guard and the database guard were removed, and only a cross-hunk read reveals it.

injection.find:
user = crud.get_user_by_email(session=session, email=user_in.email)
    if user:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=400,
            detail="The user with this email already exists in the system.",
        )

    user = crud.create_user(session=session, user_create=user_in)
